meta.title: Skill Graph: 10 Spike Results + Tech Deep Dives
meta.description: Pydanticâ†’JSONâ†’NetworkXâ†’GraphML validated. 3 independent algorithms ratify the 4-lane model. Sidecars deferred.

props.title: 10 Spikes, 3 Validations, 5 Promotions
props.subtitle: NetworkX community detection agrees with TopoNetX Euler characteristic agrees with hand-authored taxonomy. The math confirms the bones.

props.content: Codex-Ratchet absorbed. 155 nodes live. 7/7 promotion gates pass. 3 independent algorithms ratify the 4-lane taxonomy at 95%+ agreement. The heavy sidecars (PyG, clifford, LightRAG) recommend DEFER at this scale. Ship the owner stack, treat sidecars as bounded read-only projections.

props.label: THE BIG FINDING
props.title: Three Independent Validations of the 4-Lane Model
props.subtitle: Hand-authored taxonomy + community detection + CW-complex topology all converge

props.title: Validation 1: NetworkX Community Detection
props.content: **95.10% lane agreement** across Louvain, Label Propagation, and Greedy Modularity. All three algorithms find the same 4 dominant clusters on the same 155-node graph. Modularity = 0.69 (typical social graphs: 0.3-0.4). Fuzziness only at the planâ†”deliver and deliverâ†”close seams. No new lanes to discover.

props.title: Validation 2: TopoNetX Euler Characteristic
props.content: **Ď‡ = 4** from the global CW-complex: 78 nodes, 148 edges, 74 rank-2 cells. Exactly 4 contractible discs â€” one per lane. The lifecycle ring (shapeâ†’planâ†’deliverâ†’close) closes cleanly as a higher-level 2-cell. Topological proof the lane structure is well-formed.

props.title: Validation 3: Hand-Authored Taxonomy
props.content: **.lev/pm/manifests/skill-taxonomy-v1.yaml** â€” 42 external features + 68 skills mapped to 4 lanes by hand from session context. The math agrees with the author. That's the whole point: when three independent methods converge, the structure is real.

props.label: SPIKE RESULTS
props.title: 10 Spikes, All Complete
props.subtitle: Heavy sidecars â†’ DEFER. Owner stack â†’ SHIP.

props.title: 01 Gephi Lite
props.content: **USE NOW.** Client-side GraphML viewer, drag file to browser, ForceAtlas2 layout. No server. Ideal for 155-node graph.

props.title: 02 Gate Tests
props.content: **7/7 PASS in 0.31s.** Pydantic round-trip, no-sidecar-at-read, valid kinds, lanes reachable, GraphML round-trip. Gates are enforceable.

props.title: 03 PyTorch Geometric
props.content: **SKIP.** 155 nodes is below the GNN floor. Use sklearn TF-IDF + LogisticRegression with neighbor-lane histogram. <50 LOC baseline beats GNN at this scale.

props.title: 04 TopoNetX
props.content: **PROMOTE as read-only sidecar.** Ď‡=4 validates lane topology. Cheap Euler characteristic alarm detects lane degradation in future seeds.

props.title: 05 clifford
props.content: **SKIP.** numpy 2.0 pin is a non-starter in shared envs. Use `chirality: int = 0` on SkillEdge â€” 1 bit of info, not 8 floats.

props.title: 06 LightRAG
props.content: **DEFER.** 60-char descriptions are too short for embeddings. Substring search wins at 155 nodes. Revisit at >500 nodes + >200 char descs.

props.title: 07 Community Detection
props.content: **95.10% agreement** across 3 algorithms ratifies the 4-lane taxonomy. Outliers: cdo, work, arch straddle lanes â†’ candidates for Lane.CROSS.

props.title: 08 Hopf Nesting
props.content: **SIMPLIFY.** Not actually a Hopf fibration. Ship `parent_id: str | None` + `scope: ScopeLevel` enum. Recursive ancestor walk. Metaphor earns its keep only as naming discipline.

props.title: 09 Obsidian Vault
props.content: **PHASE A only.** 13,678 files in Codex-Ratchet vault. After filtering: ~6k general-knowledge nodes. Would swamp 155. Import only CROSS_VALIDATED first (200-500 nodes).

props.title: 10 Resolver
props.content: **WORKING.** `skill://work`, `lane://deliver`, `--query "plan"` all resolve. Gap: descriptions empty, needs SKILL.md frontmatter backfill in seed.py.

props.label: SHOVEL-READY
props.title: 5 Promotions, Applied Now
props.subtitle: The owner stack gets richer; the sidecars stay bounded

props.title: 01 Chirality Int
props.content: Add `chirality: int = 0` to SkillEdge. Forward=+1, reverse=-1, commutative=0. Replaces clifford library entirely for our use case.

props.title: 02 Scope Nesting
props.content: Add `parent_id` + `scope: ScopeLevel` to SkillNode. Enables skillâŠ‚laneâŠ‚workstreamâŠ‚project. Recursive ancestor walk.

props.title: 03 Description Backfill
props.content: Parse SKILL.md frontmatter in seed.py. Populate description + tags. Resolver fuzzy search actually works after this.

props.title: 04 Euler Alarm
props.content: TopoNetX sidecar runs on every seed rebuild. If Ď‡ â‰  4, flag lane degradation. Single scalar drift detector. Writes to ~/.cache/lev/harness/.

props.title: 05 sklearn Baseline
props.content: Lane classifier baseline. TF-IDF + LogisticRegression + neighbor-lane histogram. Must beat trivial majority-class before anyone installs PyTorch.

props.label: TECH DEEP DIVE Â· PROMOTED
props.title: TopoNetX â€” Topological Closure Alarm
props.subtitle: What it does, how it works, 3 concrete Lev use cases

props.content:
**What it is:** Python library for combinatorial complexes (higher-order graphs â€” edges between more than 2 nodes). Builds CellComplex, SimplicialComplex, HyperGraph from node/edge lists. Computes boundary matrices, cycles, Euler characteristic.

**Why it matters:** NetworkX only thinks in graphs (nodes+edges). TopoNetX thinks in **cells** â€” faces, volumes, higher-dimensional boundaries. For the skill graph: each operator lane is a 2-cell bounded by its skills. Lifecycle (shapeâ†’planâ†’deliverâ†’close) is itself a higher-order 2-cell over the 4 lanes.

**The payoff:** A single scalar â€” the Euler characteristic Ď‡ â€” tells you whether the topology is well-formed. Ď‡=4 means exactly 4 contractible discs (one per lane). If Ď‡ changes in a future seed, something structural broke.

**Use case 1: Lane degradation detector.** Run on every seed rebuild. If Ď‡ â‰  4, a lane lost closure (skills removed, edges broken). Automated regression check. Zero LLM calls.

**Use case 2: Workstream topology.** When we nest workstreams inside projects, each workstream becomes its own 2-cell. Compute Ď‡ for the full nested complex. Detect: "is this workstream topologically complete?" before promoting it.

**Use case 3: DNA contract coverage.** Every dna/core/*.dna.yaml module is a cell bounded by its constraints, behaviors, and failure modes. TopoNetX detects modules whose contract doesn't form a closed face â€” i.e., constraints that don't cover all behaviors.

props.label: TECH DEEP DIVE Â· CORE
props.title: NetworkX â€” The Owner Manipulation Layer
props.subtitle: Python's standard graph library; the substrate that doesn't need justification

props.content:
**What it is:** Pure Python graph library. Zero runtime deps. DiGraph, MultiDiGraph, algorithms for shortest path, community detection, centrality, traversal. Ships with GraphML, GEXF, JSON-LD, DOT export.

**Why it matters:** It's the glue between Pydantic schemas and visualization. You never query it directly for owner truth (that's in Pydantic), but every analysis passes through an `nx.DiGraph` projection.

**The payoff:** Algorithms for free. `nx.ancestors(G, 'deploy-to-prod')` â†’ full transitive dependency closure. `nx.betweenness_centrality(G)` â†’ the bridge skills that connect lanes. `nx.community.louvain_communities(G)` â†’ empirical cluster validation. All battle-tested.

**Use case 1: Transitive prerequisite resolver.** `nx.ancestors(G, 'skill://deploy')` returns every skill that must exist before deploy is executable. Powers the `lev exec` dependency check.

**Use case 2: Lane bridge finder.** `nx.betweenness_centrality` surfaces the skills that connect multiple lanes (handoff, sync, lev-align). These are the routers â€” high centrality means "many paths pass through me."

**Use case 3: DNA constraint graph traversal.** Every dna/core/*.dna.yaml constraint is a node, every `depends_on:` is an edge. NetworkX answers: what's the minimum constraint set to validate this module? Topological sort â†’ validation order.

props.label: TECH DEEP DIVE Â· DEFERRED
props.title: PyTorch Geometric â€” Graph Neural Networks
props.subtitle: Below threshold today; ready for when the graph grows

props.content:
**What it is:** PyTorch extension for GNNs. GraphSAGE, GCN, GAT, message passing, heterogeneous graphs. Used for node classification, link prediction, graph-level tasks.

**Why it's deferred:** 155 nodes is below the floor where a GNN beats trivial baselines. Sample starvation â€” we have ~60-100 labeled skills across 5 lanes. sklearn TF-IDF + LogisticRegression with a neighbor-lane histogram feature preserves the only graph signal a 2-hop GNN would capture, is inductive, interpretable, and runs in <50 LOC.

**Flip triggers:** Graph â‰Ą1k nodes, or sklearn macro-F1 < 0.6, or need for multi-edge-kind link prediction.

**Use case 1: Auto-suggest lanes during skill-builder.** When authoring a new SKILL.md, train a GNN on existing (skillâ†’lane) pairs and predict the lane for the draft. Reframe from backfill to authoring assist.

**Use case 2: Missing implements edges.** Predict which Lev skills implement which parity features (omx-02, ooo-07, etc.) when human annotation is incomplete. GNN learns patterns from the 8 known implements edges and suggests the rest.

**Use case 3: Workstream coupling predictor.** After Phase A Obsidian import (6k nodes), train a model to predict cross-workstream skill coupling. Catches architectural drift before it shows up in handoff mails.

props.label: TECH DEEP DIVE Â· SKIPPED
props.title: Clifford â€” Geometric Algebra for Edge Semantics
props.subtitle: Mathematically elegant; operationally overkill

props.content:
**What it is:** Python library for Clifford algebras (geometric algebra). Encodes rotations, reflections, and orientations as multivectors. Non-commutative by design â€” perfect for directed edges where Aâ†’B â‰  Bâ†’A.

**Why it's skipped:** The `clifford` package pins numpy 2.0.x, which conflicts with the rest of the Anaconda stack (2.4.x). GraphML can't natively serialize lists-of-floats well. And we only need ONE bit of information per edge: is this chiral or commutative? A plain `chirality: int = 0 | +1 | -1` captures that without any library.

**The lesson:** Codex-Ratchet uses clifford's full Cl(3,0) for QIT physics where grade-0/1/2/3 all matter. Skill graph only needs the pseudoscalar (grade-3) sign. 8 floats â†’ 1 int.

**Use case 1 (HYPOTHETICAL): Mirror symmetry detection.** In a knowledge graph where some relationships are mirrored (A teaches B â†” B learned from A), clifford's bivector algebra naturally handles the symmetry. Skill graph has no such symmetries; all edges are directed.

**Use case 2 (HYPOTHETICAL): Orientation-aware routing.** If we had physical meaning for edge orientation (like mesh vertex order in graphics), clifford would encode it. We don't.

**Use case 3 (HYPOTHETICAL): Adversarial edge detection.** Fancy algebraic tricks to detect edges that violate lifecycle chirality (e.g., validation â†’ alignment). But we can do this with a single if-statement: `if edge.chirality == -1 and edge.lane_transition_backwards: flag`.

props.label: TECH DEEP DIVE Â· DEFERRED
props.title: LightRAG â€” Graph-Aware Semantic Retrieval
props.subtitle: Too heavy for 60-character descriptions; back when we have real corpus

props.content:
**What it is:** Graph-aware RAG library. Indexes documents, builds a knowledge graph of entities and relations, answers queries with dual retrieval (semantic + structural). Developed by HKU.

**Why it's deferred:** Current `SkillNode.description` averages 60 characters. Embeddings on 60-char strings are mostly noise. At 155 nodes, substring search is sub-millisecond and zero-dep. LightRAG also can't reach "no sidecar at read time" â€” semantic search inherently needs vectors.

**Flip triggers:** >500 nodes, >200 char descriptions, >100 queries/day, abstract intents ("find skills for observability") that substring misses.

**Use case 1: Intent-to-skill routing.** User says "I need to audit auth flows." LightRAG indexes all skill descriptions + tags + relationships and returns ranked candidates: prompt-guard (security scan), codebase-audit (code review), defendr (attack surface). Beats manual skill-discovery.

**Use case 2: Handoff resume augmentation.** When resuming from a .lev/pm/handoffs/*.md file, LightRAG finds related skills + prior sessions + relevant specs without explicit linking. Solves the "I forgot what I was working on" problem.

**Use case 3: Cross-project knowledge transfer.** After Phase A Obsidian import, LightRAG becomes the interface for querying prior knowledge: "how did we solve auth in NAAC?" gets graph-traversal-augmented answers across projects.

props.label: TECH DEEP DIVE Â· SIMPLIFIED
props.title: Hopf Fibration â€” Nested Scopes
props.subtitle: Cool math, overkill implementation; keep the metaphor, drop the machinery

props.content:
**What it is:** A mathematical map from the 3-sphere SÂł to the 2-sphere SÂ˛, where every point in SÂ˛ corresponds to a circle in SÂł. Codex-Ratchet uses it to nest three tori (inner, Clifford, outer) for QIT physics â€” each torus is a fiber over a point in the base space.

**Why we simplified:** Our scope hierarchy (skill âŠ‚ lane âŠ‚ workstream âŠ‚ project) is just single-parent nesting. Not actually a Hopf fibration. `parent_id: str | None` + `scope: ScopeLevel` enum + recursive ancestor walk does 100% of what we need.

**What we keep:** The metaphor of oriented non-commutative nesting as naming discipline. The Hopf framing is rhetorically useful even when mathematically absent.

**Use case 1: Scope-aware queries.** `nx.ancestors(G, 'skill://work')` walks up: lane://plan â†’ workstream://skill-graph â†’ project://leviathan. Single traversal, zero library needed.

**Use case 2: Inheritance of policy.** A constraint on project://leviathan applies to all workstreams, lanes, and skills beneath it. Scope nesting enables inheritance checks: "does this skill violate any ancestor-scope constraint?"

**Use case 3: Permission boundaries.** An agent has access to a scope (e.g., workstream://browser-cascade). Any skill inside that scope is in bounds; ancestors and siblings are not. Policy enforcement via tree traversal.

props.label: NEXT
props.title: 15 More Spikes Via Lev Exec
props.subtitle: Launched as parallel subagents; each runs its own lev exec pipeline

props.content:
The owner stack is locked (Pydanticâ†’JSONâ†’NetworkXâ†’GraphML). Gates 2+3 pass. 3 independent validators agree on the 4-lane model. 5 promotions are shovel-ready.

Next session dispatches 15 more spikes via `lev exec` subagents â€” deeper integrations, real code execution, and the "/now brief for every new design" doctrine. When the sklearn baseline lands and description backfill completes, the resolver starts finding things. When TopoNetX runs on every seed, the Euler alarm catches regressions. When scope nesting lands, skill://work resolves into its full ancestry.

Ship the bones. The muscle comes next.
